content: Let $i_C, i_L$, and $i_R$ be the currents flowing through the capacitor, inductor, and resistor, respectively, in the circuit given below. The AC admittances are given in Siemens(S). Which one of the following is true?
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & I_R=1 \angle 90(0.2)=0.2 \angle 90^{\circ} \\ & I_L=1 \angle 90(0.1 \angle-90)=0.1 \angle 0^{\circ} \\ & I_C=1 \angle 90(0.25 \angle 90)=0.25 \angle 180^{\circ} \end{aligned} $$

content: A series $$RLC$$ circuit has a quality factor Q of 1000 at a center frequency of $$10^6$$ rad/s. The possible values of R, L and C are
explanation:
The quality factor (Q) of a series RLC circuit is a measure of how underdamped the circuit is. It is defined as the ratio of the resonant frequency to the bandwidth of the circuit:

$$Q = \frac{f_0}{\Delta f}$$

Where:

$$f_0$$ is the resonant frequency.

$$\Delta f$$ is the bandwidth.

The resonant frequency of a series RLC circuit is given by:

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{LC}}$$

The bandwidth of a series RLC circuit is given by:

$$\Delta f = \frac{R}{2\pi L}$$

Substituting the expressions for $$f_0$$ and $$\Delta f$$ into the equation for Q, we get:

$$Q = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{LC}} \times \frac{2\pi L}{R} = \frac{\sqrt{L}}{R\sqrt{C}}$$

Solving for R, we get:

$$R = \frac{\sqrt{L}}{Q\sqrt{C}}$$

We are given that Q = 1000, $$f_0 = 10^6$$ rad/s. We can use these values to find the possible values of R, L and C.

Let's start by assuming that L = 1 µH and C = 1 µF.

Then, we can calculate R using the formula we derived above:

$$R = \frac{\sqrt{1 \times 10^{-6}}}{1000 \sqrt{1 \times 10^{-6}}} = 0.001 \Omega$$

This corresponds to option D.

Therefore, the possible values of R, L and C are:

$$R = 0.001 \Omega, L = 1 \mu H$$ and $$C = 1 \mu F$$

So the answer is Option D.

content: The current in the RL - circuit shown below is $i(t)=10 \cos (5 t-\pi / 4) \mathrm{A}$. The value of the inductor (rounded off to two decimal places) is $\_\_\_\_$ H.
explanation:
Method 1

Given network is shown below,

$$ \begin{aligned} &i(t)=10 \cos \left(5 t-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)\\ &\text { Using sinusoidal response, } \end{aligned} $$

From the given figure,

$$ \begin{aligned} & A=200, \omega_0=5 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{sec} \\ & B=A \cdot|H(\omega)|_{\omega=\omega_0} \\ & \phi=\left.\angle H(\omega)\right|_{\omega=\omega_0} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\\ & B=10=A \cdot|H(\omega)|_{\omega=5} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\\ & \phi=\frac{-\pi}{4}=\left.\angle H(\omega)\right|_{\omega=5} \end{aligned} $$

The output current can be given as,

$$ I(s)=\frac{V_i(s)}{R+L s} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & I(s)=V_i(s) \times H(s) \\ & H(s)=\frac{1}{R+L s} \\ & H(j \omega)=\frac{1}{R+j \omega L} \\ & |H(j \omega)|=\frac{1}{\sqrt{R^2+j \omega^2 L^2}} \\ & \angle H(j \omega)=-\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega L}{R}\right) \\ & i(t)=200 \cos (5 t) \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{R^2+\omega^2 L^2}} \angle-\tan \left(\frac{\omega L}{R}\right) \end{aligned} $$

Comparing with current $i(t)$,

From equation (i),

$$ \begin{aligned} & \phi=-\left.\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega L}{R}\right)\right|_{\omega=5}=-\frac{\pi}{4} \\ & \left.\frac{\omega L}{R}\right|_{\omega=5}=\tan \frac{\pi}{4}=1 \\ & 5 \cdot L=R\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(iii) \end{aligned} $$

From equation (ii),

$$ \begin{aligned} & 10=200 \times|H(\omega)|_{\omega=5} \\ & \frac{200}{\sqrt{R^2+\omega^2 L^2}}=10 \\ & 400=R^2+25 L^2 \end{aligned} $$

From equation (iii),

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & R=5 L \\ \therefore & 400=25 L^2+25 L^2 \\ & L=\sqrt{\frac{400}{50}}=2.828 \mathrm{H} \end{array} $$

Method 2

$$ \begin{aligned} & Z=\frac{V}{I}=\frac{200 \angle 0^0}{10 \angle-45^0}=20 \angle 45^0 \\ & Z=10 \sqrt{2}+j 10 \sqrt{2} \\ & X_L=10 \sqrt{2}=\omega L \\ & L=\frac{10 \sqrt{2}}{5}=2 \sqrt{2}=2.828 \mathrm{H} \end{aligned} $$

content: In the circuit shown below, all the components are ideal and the input voltage is sinusoidal. The magnitude of the steady - state output $V_0$ (rounded off to two decimal places) is $\_\_\_\_$ V.
explanation:
Method 1

Given circuit is shown below,

$$ \begin{aligned} & V_{r m s}=\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \\ & V_m=\sqrt{2} V_{r m s}=\sqrt{2} \times 230 \mathrm{~V} \\ & V_m=230 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned} $$

Also given input voltage is sinusoidal.

$$ \begin{aligned} & V_{i n}=V_m \sin \omega t \\ & V_{i n}=230 \sqrt{2} \sin \omega t \end{aligned} $$

Assume that negative half cycle occurs first,

Case 1: During $1^{\text {st }}$ negative half cycle,

Diode $D_1$ will be forward biased and diode $D_2$ will be reverse biased.

Hence, charging of capacitor $C_1$ will start and it will charge upto maximum value of input.

Hence, $=230 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~V}$

$$ V_{C_1}=|230 \sqrt{2} \sin \omega t|=230 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~V} \text { and } V_{C_2}=0 \mathrm{~V} $$

Case 2 : During $1{ }^{\text {st }}$ positive half cycle,

Diode $D_1$ will be reverse biased and diode $D_2$ will be forward biased.

Hence, charging of capacitor $C_2$ will start and capacitor $C_2$ will charge upto maximum value of input, which appear across capacitor $C_2$.

$$ \begin{aligned} & V_{C_2}=V_{C_1}+|230 \sqrt{2} \sin \omega t|_{\max } \\ & V_{C_2}=230 \sqrt{2}+230 \sqrt{2} \\ & V_{C_2}=460 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned} $$

Case 3:

During $2^{\text {nd }}$ positive half cycle, negative terminal of diode $D_1$ is at $230 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~V}$ and negative terminal of $D_2$ is at $460 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~V}$. Now, diode $D_1$ and $D_2$ will be forward bias when voltage at their positive terminal will be higher than that of the negative terminal, which is never possible beyond first negative half cycle and first positive half cycle.

$$ \text { Hence, in this case, } D_1 \text { and } D_2 \text { will be reverse biased. } $$

Hence, from above circuit

$$ \begin{aligned} & V_0=V_{C_2}=460 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~V} \\ & V_0=650.538 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the magnitude of the steady-state output $V_0$ is $460 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~V}$.

Method 2

Given circuit is shown below,

$$ \text { The clamper will do the shift of input voltage to } 2 V_m \text {. } $$

Given rms value of sinusoidal input

$$ \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}=230 \Rightarrow V_m=\sqrt{2} \times 230 $$

Peak detector will detect the maximum value $=2 V_m=2 \times 230(\sqrt{2})=650.53$

Method 3

Define the circuit as a voltage doubler.

$$ V_0=2 V_m=2 \times 230 \sqrt{2}=650.4 \mathrm{~V} $$

A series $$R$$-$$L$$-$$C$$ circuit has a $$Q$$ of $$100$$ and an impedance of $$\left( {100 + j0} \right)\,\Omega $$ at its resonant angular frequency of $${10^7}$$ rad/sec. The values of $$R$$ and $$L$$ are: $$R=$$ ______ ohms. $$L=$$ ________Henries.

The current $$i(t)$$ through a $$10$$-$$\Omega $$ resistor in series with an inductance is given by
$$i(t)$$$$ = 3 + 4\sin \left( {100t + {{45}^ \circ }} \right) + 4\sin \left( {300t + {{60}^ \circ }} \right)\,\,A$$.

The RMS value of the current and the power dissipated in the circuit are
explanation:
Given : $$i(t) = 3 + 4\sin (100t + 45^\circ ) + 4\sin (300t + 60^\circ )$$

RMS value of i(t) is given by,

$${I_{rms}} = \sqrt {{{(3)}^2} + {{\left( {{4 \over {\sqrt 2 }}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {{4 \over {\sqrt 2 }}} \right)}^2}} $$

$${I_{rms}} = \sqrt {9 + 8 + 8} = 5A$$

Power dissipated in 10$$\Omega$$ resistor is given by,

$$P = I_{rms}^2R = {(5)^2} \times 10 = 250\,W$$

Hence, the correct option is (C).

What should be the value of the variable capacitor $C$ such that the RMS readings on $M_1$ and $M_2$ are 25 V and 5 A , respectively?
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} X_L & =\omega L=200 \times 1=200 \Omega \\ Z & =\frac{V}{I}=\frac{25}{5}=5 \Omega \\ Z & =R+j\left(X_L-X_C\right) \\ X_L & =X_C \\ X_L & =X_C=200 \Omega \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} \frac{1}{\omega C} & =200 \\ \frac{1}{200 \times C} & =200 \\ C & =\frac{1}{200 \times 200}=25 \mu \mathrm{~F} \end{aligned} $$

content: For the circuit shown, the locus of the impedance Z(j$$\omega$$) is plotted as $$\omega$$ increases from zero to infinity. The values of R1 and R2 are :
explanation:
At $$\omega$$ = 0 rad/s,

XC = $${1 \over {\omega C}} = \infty $$

Impedance, Z(0) = R1 + R2

With the help of graph at $$\omega$$ = 0.

Z(0) = 5 k$$\Omega$$

R1 + R2 = 5 k$$\Omega$$

At $$\omega$$ = $$\infty$$ rad/s, XC = $${1 \over {\omega C}}$$

XC = 0

Impedance, Z($$\infty$$) = R1 = 2 k$$\Omega$$

R1 + R2 = 5 k$$\Omega$$

R2 = 3 k$$\Omega$$

content: Consider the circuit shown in the figure with input V(t) in volts. The sinusoidal steady state current I(t) flowing through the circuit is shown graphically (where t is in seconds). The circuit element Z can be _________.
explanation:
As the current i(t) is lagging, element Z is inductor,

$$I = {V \over {{Z_0}}}$$

$$I = {{\sin t} \over {{Z_0}}}$$

Maximum value of current $$i(t) = {1 \over {\sqrt 2 }}$$

$$\therefore$$ $$\left| {{Z_0}} \right| = \sqrt 2 $$

$${Z_0} = R + j{X_L} = 1 + j\omega L$$

$$\sqrt {1 + {\omega ^2}{L^2}} = \sqrt 2 $$

$$1 + {\omega ^2}{L^2} = 2$$

given, $$\omega$$ = 1 rad/sec

L2 = 1

L = 1 H

content: $$ \text { The current } I \text { in the given network is } $$
explanation:
$$ \text { Given circuit is shown in below figure, } $$

$$ Z_L=80-j 35=87.32 \angle-23.63^{\circ} $$

From figure, $\quad-V_1+I_1 Z_L=0$

$$ \begin{aligned} & I_1=\frac{V_1}{Z_L}=\frac{120 \angle-90^0}{87.32 \angle-23.63^0} \\ & I_1=1.374 \angle-66.37^0 \end{aligned} $$

Similarly,   $$ \begin{aligned} & -V_2+I_2 Z_L=0 \\ & I_2=\frac{V_2}{Z_L}=\frac{-120 \angle-30^0}{87.32 \angle-23.68^0} \\ & \quad=-1.374 \angle-6.32^0 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned}From \,\,\,circuit,   & I+I_1=I_2 \\ & I=I_2-I_1 \\ & I=\left(-1.374 \angle-6.32^0\right)-\left(1.374 \angle-66.37^0\right) \\ & I=(-1.36+0.341 j)-(0.55-1.258 j) \\ & I=-1.91+1.408 j \\ & I=2.373 \angle 143.60^0 \mathrm{~A} \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct option is (D).

content: For the circuit given in the figure, the voltage VC (in volts) across the capacitor is :

In the circuit shown the voltage $${V_{IN}}\,\left( t \right)$$ is described by: $$${V_{IN}}\,\left( t \right) = \left\{ {\matrix{ {0,} & {for\,\,\,t < 0} \cr {15Volts,} & {for\,\,\,t \ge 0} \cr } } \right.$$$

where $$'t'$$ is in seconds. The time (in seconds) at which the current $${\rm I}$$ in the circuit will reach the value $$2$$ Ampere is ______ .

The figure shows an RLC circuit excited by the sinusoidal voltage $$100cos(3t)$$ Volts, where $$t$$ is in seconds. The ratio $${{amplitude\,\,of\,\,{V_2}} \over {amplitude\,\,of\,\,{V_1}}}\,\,$$ is ________ .

An AC voltage source V = 10 sin(t) volts is applied to the following network. Assume that R1 = 3 kΩ, R2 = 6 kΩ and R3 = 9 kΩ and that the diode is ideal.

RMS current Irms (in mA) through the diode is _________.
explanation:
The current through $R_1$ is

$$ I_{\mathrm{R} 1}=\left(\frac{I_{\mathrm{rms}}}{3}\right) $$

The current through $R_2$ is

$$ I_{\mathrm{R} 2}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\left(\frac{I_{\mathrm{rms}}}{3}\right)=\left(\frac{I_{\mathrm{rms}}}{6}\right) $$

The current through $R_3$ is

$$ I_{\mathrm{R} 3}=\left(\frac{I_{\mathrm{rms}}}{6}\right)+\left(\frac{I_{\mathrm{rms}}}{6}\right)=\left(\frac{I_{\mathrm{rms}}}{3}\right) $$

Let RMS current $I_{\mathrm{rms}}=I$

Resistance $R_1=3 \mathrm{k} \Omega$

Resistance $R_2=6 \mathrm{k} \Omega$

Resistance $R_3=9 \mathrm{k} \Omega$,

$$ \begin{aligned} V &=\frac{I}{3}\left[R_1\right]+\frac{I}{6}\left[R_2\right]+\frac{I}{3} R_3 \\\\ &=\frac{I}{3}[3]+\frac{I}{6}[6]+\frac{I}{3}[9] \\\\ V &=I[5 \Omega] \\\\ \frac{V}{I} &=5 \Omega \end{aligned} $$

For half weve rectifier

$$ \begin{aligned} &I_{\text {rms}}=\frac{I_m}{(2)}=\frac{10 \sin t}{5 k \Omega}=2 \sin t \mathrm{~mA} \\\\ &\therefore I_{\mathrm{rms}}=\frac{I_m}{2}=1 \mathrm{~mA} \end{aligned} $$

content:
A network consisting of a finite number of linear resistor (R), inductor (L), and capacitor (C) elements, connected all in series or all in parallel, is excited with a source of the form
$$\sum\limits_{k = 1}^3 {{a_k}\,\,\cos \,\left( {k{\omega _0}t} \right),\,\,\,} $$ where $${a_k} \ne 0,\,\,{\omega _0} \ne 0$$.

The source has nonzero impedance. Which one of the following is a possible form of the output measured across a resistor in the network?
In the given circuit, the maximum power (in Watts) that can be transferred to the load RL is ___________________.
explanation:
Given circuit is shown below,

1. Calculation of $V_{T H}$ :

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Applying KVL in loop shown, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & I=\frac{4 \angle 0^0}{2+j 2}=\frac{2}{(1+j)}=\sqrt{2} \angle-45^0 \mathrm{~A} \\ & V_{T H}=j 2 I=j 2 \times \sqrt{2} \angle-45^0 \mathrm{~V} \\ & V_{T H}=2 \sqrt{2} \angle 45^0 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

2. Calculation of $Z_{T H}$ :

(when dependent sources are not present) Replace all independent source by their internal resistance, i.e. short circuit all the independent voltage source ( $R_{\text {in }}=0$ ) and open circuit all the independent current source ( $R_{i n}=\infty$ ).

$$\begin{aligned} & Z_{T H}=2 \|(j 2)=\frac{2 \times j 2}{2+j 2}=\frac{j 2}{(1+j)} \\ & Z_{T H}=\frac{2 \angle 90^0}{\sqrt{2} \angle 45^0}=\sqrt{2} \angle 45^0=(1+j) \Omega \end{aligned}$$

Thevenin equivalent circuit:

In case of complex network, maximum power will be transferred to $R_L$, when

$$R_L=\left|Z_{T H}\right|=\sqrt{1^2+1^2}=\sqrt{2} \Omega\quad$$ [Refer table 2.1]

Load current is given by,

$$\begin{aligned} & I_L=\frac{V_{T H}}{R_L+Z_{T H}}=\frac{2 \sqrt{2} \angle 45^0}{\sqrt{2}+\sqrt{2} \angle 45^0} \\ & I_L=\frac{2+j 2}{\sqrt{2}+1+j}=\frac{2 \angle 45^0}{1.85 \angle 22.6^0} \\ & I_L=1.08 \angle 22.4^0 \mathrm{~A} \\ & \left|I_L\right|=1.08 \mathrm{~A} \\ & P_{\max }=\left|I_L^2\right| R_L=(1.08)^2 \times \sqrt{2}=1.65 \mathrm{~W} \end{aligned}$$

Hence, the maximum power that can be transferred to the load $R_L$ is $\mathbf{1 . 6 5 ~ W}$.

content: The damping ratio of a series $$RLC$$ circuit can be expressed as
In the circuit shown, the current $${\rm I}$$ flowing through the $$50\,\Omega $$ resistor will be zero if the value of capacitor C (in $$\mu F$$) is ________ .

A series LCR circuit is operated at a frequency different from its resonant frequency. The
operating frequency is such that the current leads the supply voltage. The magnitude of
current is half the value at resonance. If the values of L, C and R are 1 H, 1 F and 1Ω ,
respectively, the operating angular frequency (in rad/s) is ________.
A periodic variable x is shown in the figure as a function of time. The root - mean - square (rms) value of x is _______ .
explanation:
$$ \text { Given waveform is shown below, } $$

For the given signal,

$$ x(t)= \begin{cases}\frac{2}{T} t, & 0 < t \leq \frac{T}{2} \\ 0, & \frac{T}{2} < t \leq T\end{cases} $$

RMS value of periodic waveform is given by,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { RMS value }=\sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_0^T x^2(t) d t} \\ & x_{r m s}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\left[\int_0^{T / 2}\left(\frac{2}{T} t\right)^2 d t+\int_{T / 2}^T(0)^2 d t\right]} \\ & x_{r m s}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_0^{T / 2} \frac{4}{T^2} t^2 d t}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \times \frac{4}{T^2} \times\left[\frac{t^3}{3}\right]_0^{T / 2}} \\ & x_{r m s}=\sqrt{\frac{4}{T^3} \times \frac{T^3}{24}}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{6}}=0.408 \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the root-mean-square (rms) value of $x$ is 0.408.

If the 3-phase balanced source in Fig. delivers 1500 W at a leading power factor of 0.844, then the value of ZL (in ohm) is approximately
explanation:
Power delivered in a 3-phase balanced source,

$$ P=3 V_{\mathrm{p}} I_{\mathrm{p}} \cos \phi $$

$$ \begin{gathered} 3 \times\left(\frac{V_{\mathrm{L}}}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \times\left(\frac{V_{\mathrm{L}}}{\sqrt{3} Z_{\mathrm{L}}}\right) \times \cos \phi=1500 \\\\ Z_{\mathrm{L}}=\frac{V_L^2 \cos \phi}{1500}=\frac{(400)^2 \times 0.844}{1500}=90 \Omega \\\\ \cos \phi=0.844 \Rightarrow \phi=\cos ^{-1}(0.844)=32.44^{\circ} \end{gathered} $$

As power factor is leading so load will be capacitive and, hence, angle will be negative.

$$ Z_{\mathrm{L}}=90 \angle-32.44^{\circ} $$

content:
The transfer function of simple RC network functioning as a controller is $$$G\,{}_c\left( s \right)\,\,\,\,{{s + {z_1}} \over {s + {p_1}}}$$$

The condition for the RC network to act as a phase lead controller is
The half - power bandwidth of the resonant circuit of figure can be increased by
explanation:
Selectivity $\propto Q$

$$ \begin{aligned} Q &=\frac{f}{B \cdot W} \text { or } B \cdot W \quad \propto \frac{1}{Q} \end{aligned} $$

$B . W \propto \frac{1}{\text { selectivity }}$

If $R_1 \rightarrow 0$ and $R_1 \rightarrow \infty$

then circuit will have only $L$ and $C$ element and has selectivity.

Half power bandwidth can be reduced by decreasing selectivity and vice versa.

So, by increasing series resistance $R_1$ and decreasing parallel resistance $R_2$ the half power bandwidth can be increased.

content: The value of current through the 1F capacitor of figure is.
explanation:
Given circuit is shown below,



$${Z_1} = 1 + {2 \over s} = 1 + {2 \over {j\omega }}$$

$${Z_2} = 1 + {2 \over s} = 1 + {2 \over {j\omega }}$$

$${Z_3} = 1\Omega ,\,{Z_4} = 1\Omega $$

Above circuit is a balanced bridge since,

$$\left[ {{{{Z_1}} \over {{Z_3}}} = {{{Z_2}} \over {{Z_4}}}} \right]$$

So, the current through the diagonal element i.e. 1 F capacitor is zero.

content:
For network shown in Fig. $$R\, = \,1\,k\Omega $$
$${L_1} = 2\,H,\,{L_2} = 5\,H,\,{L_3}\, = \,1H,{L_4} = 4H\,\,\,$$ and $$C - 0.2\,\,\mu F.$$. The mutual inductances are $${M_{12}} = 3\,H$$ and $${M_{34}} = 2\,H$$.

Determine
(a) the equivalent inductance for the combination of $${L_3}$$ and $${L_4}$$,
(b) the equivalent inductance across the points A and B in the network,
(c) the resonant frequency of the network.

answer:
E2 = 8.77 ej17.9o V
E0 = 1.96 e-j81.3o V
I = 6.202 e-j9.9o A

content:
For the circuit in Fig. Which is in steady state,

(a)Find the frequency $${\omega _0}$$ at which the magnitude of the impedance across terminals a, b reaches maximum.

(b) Find the impedance across a, b at the frequency $${\omega _0}$$.

(c) If $${v_i}\left( t \right) = V\,\,\sin \left( {{\omega _0}t} \right),$$ find $${i_L}\left( t \right),\,\,{i_c}\left( t \right),{i_R}\left( t \right).$$
answer:
(a) $${W_0}\, = {1 \over 4}\,\,\,r/s$$

(b) $$4\Omega $$

(c) $${i_L}\left( t \right)\, = \, - {V \over 2}cos\left( {{1 \over 4}t} \right)\,\,{\rm A}$$
$${i_C}\left( t \right)\, = \,{V \over 2}cos\left( {{1 \over 4}t} \right)\,\,{\rm A},$$ $${i_R}\left( t \right)\, = \,{2 \over 4}sin\left( {{1 \over 4}t} \right)\,\,{\rm A}$$

content: A coil with a quality factor $$(Q)$$ of $$10$$ is put in series with a capacitor $${C_1}$$ of $$10\,\,\mu F,$$ and the combination is found to draw maximum current when a sinusoidal voltage of frequency $$50$$ $$Hz$$ is applied. A second capacitor $${C_2}$$ is now in parallel with the circuit.
